questions.text: What does a 'Decreasing Term Assurance' policy primarily cover?
questions.explanation: Decreasing term insurance is typically tied to a loan (like a home loan). The sum assured decreases every year, matching the outstanding loan balance.

questions.text: Under which section of the Income Tax Act are the death benefits of a life insurance policy completely tax-free?
questions.explanation: Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act ensures that the maturity and death claims received from a standard life insurance policy are exempt from tax.

questions.text: Which document is issued when the original policy bond is lost or destroyed?
questions.explanation: If the original policy document is lost, the insurer issues a Duplicate Policy after obtaining an indemnity bond and required fees from the policyholder.

questions.text: What is the primary feature of an 'Annuity Certain'?
options: It pays out only upon death
It pays a regular income for a fixed number of years, regardless of whether the annuitant lives or dies
It pays a lump sum after 10 years
questions.explanation: In an Annuity Certain, the pension amount is guaranteed to be paid for a specific period (e.g., 5, 10, or 15 years), even if the annuitant passes away during this period.

questions.text: Who acts as the 'First Underwriter' in the life insurance business?
questions.explanation: The agent is the primary and first underwriter because they personally meet the prospect, observe their lifestyle, and submit the Agent's Confidential Report (ACR).

questions.text: As per the Consumer Protection Act, what is the pecuniary jurisdiction of a State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission?
options: Between Rs. 50 Lakhs and Rs. 2 Crores
questions.explanation: According to updated Consumer Protection rules, the State Commission handles complaints where the value of goods/services exceeds Rs. 50 Lakhs but does not exceed Rs. 2 Crores.

questions.text: In a Life Insurance contract, who is the 'Assignor'?
options: The person who transfers the rights of the policy to another party
The person who receives the rights of the policy
questions.explanation: The Assignor is the original policyholder who executes the assignment deed to transfer their ownership rights to another entity (the Assignee).

questions.text: If a claim is repudiated (rejected) by the insurance company, what must the company provide to the claimant?
options: A refund of the last premium
A written letter clearly stating the specific reasons for repudiation
questions.explanation: Insurance companies must raise all requirements or queries regarding a claim at one go and within 30 days of receiving the claim notice.

questions.text: What does an 'Alteration' mean in policy servicing?
options: Making changes to the policy terms, like mode of premium or sum assured, after it has been issued
Transferring the policy to another company
Taking a loan against the policy
questions.explanation: Alterations refer to authorized modifications requested by the policyholder, such as changing the premium payment frequency from yearly to monthly.

questions.text: What is the primary condition for an 'Insurable Interest' to be valid in life insurance?
options: The proposer must be blood-related to the insured
The proposer must suffer a financial loss upon the death of the insured
The proposer must be older than the insured
The proposer and insured must live in the same house
questions.explanation: Insurable interest exists when the proposer derives a financial benefit from the continued life of the insured and would suffer a financial loss upon their death.

questions.text: Which type of life insurance policy is usually taken by a company to cover the risk of losing a crucial employee?
questions.explanation: Keyman Insurance protects a business from the financial shock and loss of profits that would occur if a vital, key employee dies.

questions.text: What is the full form of 'FPR'?
questions.explanation: First Premium Receipt (FPR) is the official receipt issued when the first premium is paid and the underwriter accepts the risk. It confirms the contract has started.

questions.text: Which document is considered a 'Non-Standard' proof of age?
questions.explanation: A declaration by an elder or a self-declaration is considered non-standard. Passports, PAN cards, and driving licenses are official, standard proofs.

questions.text: A life insurance policy where the premium is paid only once at the beginning of the contract is called:
questions.explanation: In a Single Premium policy, the entire premium for the policy term is paid upfront in one lump sum.

questions.text: According to IRDAI guidelines, what is the maximum time a customer has to cancel a policy under the 'Free Look Period'?
options: 7 days from the date of proposal
15 days from the date of receipt of the policy document
30 days from the date of policy issuance
45 days from the date of agent meeting
questions.explanation: The 15-day Free Look Period officially begins on the exact date the policyholder receives the physical or electronic policy document.

questions.text: What is the primary role of the 'Insurance Ombudsman'?
options: To provide a quick, informal, and cost-free mechanism for resolving customer grievances
questions.explanation: The Ombudsman acts as an alternate dispute resolution body to quickly settle policyholder complaints regarding claims or services without court fees.

questions.text: Which of the following falls under the category of 'Financial Underwriting'?
options: Checking the BMI of the applicant
Assessing whether the applicant's income justifies the requested Sum Assured
Checking the family history of heart disease
questions.explanation: Financial underwriting ensures the requested life cover is proportionate to the applicant's earning capacity, preventing over-insurance and moral hazard.

questions.text: If a person buys a life insurance policy and commits suicide within 6 months, what does the nominee generally receive?
options: 80% of the premiums paid till the date of death
questions.explanation: Under the standard suicide clause, if suicide happens within 12 months of inception, the nominee is refunded 80% of the total premiums paid.

questions.text: What does the 'Principle of Indemnity' ensure?
options: The insured gets back all premiums at maturity
The insured cannot make a profit from an insurance claim
The insured can claim from multiple life insurance companies
questions.explanation: Indemnity applies to general/health insurance. It ensures compensation matches the exact financial loss, preventing the insured from profiting from a disaster.

questions.text: In the context of AML (Anti-Money Laundering), what does 'KYC' stand for?
questions.explanation: Know Your Customer (KYC) is the mandatory process of verifying the identity and address of the applicant to prevent financial fraud and money laundering.

questions.text: Which life insurance plan provides regular periodic payouts during the policy term and a lump sum at maturity?
questions.explanation: Money Back policies provide periodic 'survival benefits' (e.g., 20% of sum assured every 5 years) while keeping the life cover intact until maturity.

questions.text: What does a 'Unit Linked Insurance Plan' (ULIP) combine?
options: Life insurance protection and market-linked investment
Term insurance and a fixed deposit
questions.explanation: ULIPs offer a dual benefit: a portion of the premium goes towards providing life cover, while the rest is invested in market funds chosen by the customer.

questions.text: What is 'Churning' in the insurance industry?
options: An agent unethically advising a client to replace an existing policy with a new one solely to earn a fresh commission
questions.explanation: Churning (or Twisting) is an unethical sales practice that harms the customer, as they lose the benefits and acquired value of their old policy.

questions.text: Who regulates the operations of Insurance Brokers in India?
questions.explanation: The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) is the sole regulatory body for all insurance intermediaries, including agents and brokers.

questions.text: What is an 'Early Claim' in life insurance?
options: A claim filed within the first month
A claim where death occurs within 3 years from the date of policy inception or revival
A claim filed before paying the first premium
questions.explanation: Any death claim that arises within 3 years of risk commencement or revival is categorized as an Early Claim and is subject to intense investigation for fraud.

questions.text: Under Section 39 of the Insurance Act, a nomination can be made in favor of:
options: Any individual, but naming a minor requires an appointee
questions.explanation: A policyholder can nominate anyone. However, if the nominee is a minor, an adult appointee must be designated to receive the money on the minor's behalf.

questions.text: What happens to an existing nomination when a policy is assigned absolutely to a bank?
options: The nominee shares the money with the bank
questions.explanation: An absolute assignment transfers full ownership rights. Under law, executing an absolute assignment automatically cancels any prior nomination.

questions.text: Which type of risk cannot be insured?
questions.explanation: Speculative risks (like gambling or investing in shares) where there is a chance of making a profit cannot be insured. Insurance only covers Pure Risks (chance of loss only).

questions.text: What is the 'Sum Assured' in a life insurance policy?
options: The guaranteed amount payable by the insurer on the occurrence of the insured event
The market value of the policy
questions.explanation: The Sum Assured is the fundamental guaranteed amount stated in the policy contract that forms the basis of the death or maturity payout.

questions.text: What is a 'Rider'?
options: A free gift with the policy
An add-on benefit attached to the base policy for an additional premium
The person who delivers the policy
questions.explanation: Riders are customizable add-ons (like Accidental Death or Critical Illness cover) that enhance the base policy's protection at a nominal extra cost.

questions.text: In Health Insurance, what does 'TPA' stand for?
questions.explanation: A Third Party Administrator (TPA) is a specialized agency licensed by IRDAI to process health insurance claims and manage cashless hospital networks.

questions.text: A policyholder stops paying premiums after 4 years on a 20-year endowment policy. What happens to the policy?
options: It is cancelled without any value
It becomes a 'Paid-up' policy with a reduced Sum Assured
The insurer pays the remaining premiums
questions.explanation: Traditional policies acquire a surrender value after 2-3 years. If premiums stop after that, the policy doesn't lapse completely but continues as a 'Paid-up' policy with reduced benefits.

questions.text: What does the 'Principle of Subrogation' imply?
options: Transferring the policy to a minor
The insurer stepping into the shoes of the insured to recover the loss from a guilty third party after paying the claim
questions.explanation: Subrogation allows the insurance company to sue the party responsible for the damage to recover the money they paid out to their insured.

questions.text: Which document legally confirms the changes made in a policy contract?
questions.explanation: An endorsement is a written note or stamp attached to the policy document that legally validates any change, like a change in address or nominee.

questions.text: If an applicant has a hazardous occupation (like mining), what action will the underwriter most likely take?
questions.explanation: Occupational hazards increase the risk of death. The underwriter will classify this as a sub-standard risk and charge an extra premium to cover the increased liability.

questions.text: Which of the following describes 'Adverse Selection'?
options: Individuals with a higher probability of loss actively seeking insurance at standard rates
The customer choosing a bad policy
questions.explanation: Adverse (or Anti) selection occurs when sick or high-risk people try to buy insurance without disclosing their true condition, unfairly increasing the insurer's risk.

questions.text: Under the Married Women's Property (MWP) Act, who can a married man name as beneficiaries?
options: His wife alone, children alone, or both jointly
questions.explanation: Section 6 of the MWP Act restricts beneficiaries exclusively to the policyholder's wife and/or children, protecting the policy amount from creditors.

questions.text: What does a 'Reversionary Bonus' mean?
options: A bonus paid immediately in cash
A bonus declared annually and added to the policy account, payable at maturity or death
questions.explanation: Reversionary bonuses are declared by participating policies. They are not paid out immediately but accumulate ('vest') and are paid along with the final claim.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of 'Underwriting'?
options: To fairly assess and price the risk, preventing anti-selection
To reject as many proposals as possible
questions.explanation: The core function of an underwriter is to evaluate the risk profile of an applicant to ensure they are charged a premium that accurately reflects their risk level.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a 'Material Fact'?
options: Applicant works as a deep-sea diver
questions.explanation: Material facts are details that directly affect mortality risk. Dietary preferences (unless tied to a medical condition) are generally not material facts.

questions.text: According to Section 45 of the Insurance Act, after how many years from issuance can an insurer NOT question a life insurance policy on any grounds?
questions.explanation: The amended Section 45 (Indisputability Clause) strictly states that no life insurance policy can be called into question on any grounds after 3 years from the date of issuance.

questions.text: What does 'Ex Gratia' payment refer to?
options: A voluntary payment made by the insurer out of goodwill, even when there is no strict legal liability
questions.explanation: Ex Gratia means 'by favor'. The insurer pays a portion or all of a claim on humanitarian grounds, even if the claim is technically excluded under policy terms.

questions.text: What happens when a life insurance policy 'Matures'?
options: The term of the contract ends, and the surviving policyholder receives the agreed payout
The policy is cancelled due to non-payment
questions.explanation: Maturity occurs when an endowment or savings policy successfully reaches the end of its stipulated term, and the insurer pays the maturity benefit to the living policyholder.

questions.text: Which body formulated the Code of Conduct that every insurance agent must follow?
questions.explanation: IRDAI strictly prescribes the Code of Conduct, which outlines the ethical and professional standards required of every licensed insurance agent.

questions.text: What is the primary function of a 'Medical Examiner' in the underwriting process?
options: To provide medical treatment to the applicant
To assess the applicant's health and provide a factual medical report to the underwriter
questions.explanation: The medical examiner acts as the eyes of the underwriter, conducting tests and reporting the physical health status of the applicant.

questions.text: If a nominee dies before the life assured, what is the status of the nomination?
options: It transfers to the nominee's heirs
It becomes void, and the policyholder can appoint a new nominee
The money goes to the government
questions.explanation: Nomination is valid only if the nominee survives the policyholder. If the nominee predeceases, the nomination fails, and the right reverts to the policyholder.

questions.text: What does a 'Grace Period' in insurance prevent?
options: Immediate lapse of the policy if a premium is paid slightly late
The agent from leaving the company
The insurer from changing the premium
The policyholder from changing the nominee
questions.explanation: The grace period provides an extra window (15 or 30 days) after the due date to pay the premium without losing life cover or policy benefits.

questions.text: In Unit Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs), the investment risk is borne by:
questions.explanation: Unlike traditional plans, ULIPs link returns to market performance. Since the policyholder chooses the funds (Equity/Debt), they bear the entire investment risk.

questions.text: Which concept calculates the exact economic value of a person to their family based on future earnings?
questions.explanation: Human Life Value is the fundamental concept developed by Prof. S.S. Huebner used to objectively determine how much life insurance cover a person actually needs.

questions.text: What is 'Rebating' as defined under Section 41 of the Insurance Act?
options: Paying a lower premium due to good health
An agent offering a part of their commission to the customer as an inducement to buy a policy
A bonus declared by the insurer
questions.explanation: Rebating is an illegal and punishable act where an agent bribes or induces a prospect by sharing their first-year commission.
